Purpose of Life Story Session
(SI Perspective)
 	Gather crucial information
 	Know who is in client’s life
 	Important relatives and friends
 	Key Decisions person may have made
 	Life stages that may have been skipped
 	Current life stage
 	Mode of being person most often operates from

-- 3 of 20 --

Elevation Strategy
Anything that the client reveals to the coach is subject to
an emotional interpretation – as either good or bad, for
instance. This is in the coach’s control.
Use the Elevation strategy to find the good intention and
strength in what your client reveals. This builds trust and
will help you discover the client’s strengths that will
enable them to overcome any challenges now.
Elevation strategy – build stronger positive intent,
stronger identity, stronger history, stronger possibilities.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 20 --

Elevation Strategy
Whenever it is possible practice elevating the clients
beliefs about themselves and their story.
Examples:
“I stayed home and took care of my little sister all the
time.” – client
“You must have been really mature to do this.” – coach
“No my mom was just irresponsible.”- client
“I’m sure there was a strong part of you that was there for
your sister.” - coach
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 20 --

Life Stages
All requests for coaching take place within a context of a
Life Stage issue. Throughout life, we are confronted with
new situations, needs, and challenges based on our age,
health, relationship cycle, professional development, etc.
One day you can wake up in a new life stage where there’s
something you want and need today that you didn’t feel
you needed yesterday. That’s how life changes for us.
By understanding a client’s life stage, the coach can
discover 1 to 3 priorities that would significantly change
the client’s life. Find these, and you’ll know what to do.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 20 --

Life Stages: Leaving Home
The young person leaving home has 3 main priorities:
1) get basic life skills for living away from home
2) find friends, peers, social life and/or relationship
3) set upon some course of learning or life direction (i.e.
school, job, or compelling goal)
If a young person succeeds at these, they will be set up
for their next life stage. If they fail at these now, this may
hold them back later. The rest is just details!
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 20 --

Life Stages: Late Career Athlete
The pro athlete in their late 30’s has 3 main priorities:
1) Set aside earnings from peak earning years
2) Reset their priorities and needs so that they draw
satisfaction and fulfillment from post-athletic life
3) Capitalize on prestige and reputation to build new
career opportunities and profit centers.
If a late-stage athlete succeeds at these, they will be set
up for their next life stage. If they fail at these now, this
may hold them back later.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 20 --

Key Decision
When listening to a client, you will hear about significant events
in their life – where they most likely made a key decision. Key
decisions are broad, general decisions that affect the rest of
your life.
Examples of key decisions:
“I’m not a people person” vs. “I am a very social person.”
“I value family above all else” vs. “My career is everything.”
“You can’t trust people with your feelings” vs. “My life is an
open book.”
“I enjoy every day to the fullest” vs. “When ____ succeeds, I
will get ____.”
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 20 --

Finding the Spark
As a coach, you need to believe that, whatever challenge
your client is dealing with, they have the emotional
resource to solve the problem.
The shy person can in certain contexts be funny.
The scared person has resources to be courageous.
In the Finding the Spark Strategy, you look for the spark:
moments of enthusiasm, desire, humor, interest, passion,
even anger, which can guide you to the person’s true
emotional resources.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 20 --

Finding the Spark
 	Listen for a clients enthusiastic tone when they talk
about a past experience, activity or kind of relationship.
You can say to them, “I really felt that excitement in you.
How could you know experience that more. I know you
won’t be singing in the garage like you did at 16 but what
could you do that is social and involves music?”
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 20 --

Bright Spot Strategy
When your client tells their story, they may touch on
moments when their needs were met in a stronger way.
When you find something that works, we call that a
“bright spot” and strive to understand what made it work.
When someone reminisces about moments when they felt
more connected to certain activities, relationships, or
their identity – ask more about it so you understand it.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 20 --

Mode of Being
How you interact with the world.
Examples:
Team Player
Leader
Observer
Creative
Critique
Learner
Philosopher
Romantic
Your preferred way of
experiencing and
participating in life,
events, school, work and
social gatherings.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 20 --

Life Story Intro to Client
“I would like to cover a lot today about your life. The
reason I do this is to understand as much as I can about
you, the important events from your life, and the people
who were and are key players. In Strategic Intervention
most of our coaching is centered in the “now,” but I want
to make sure I get a full clear picture of who you are and
what your journey has been like. I’ll start by asking you
questions and we will see where it takes us. Sound good?”
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 20 --

Questions you may want to ask
Tell me a little about your family growing up?
Do you have siblings?
Where did you live?
What was your home like?
Would you say your younger years had a theme?
Was their a person in your life a friend or family member
who gave you a lot of wisdom, magic, or peace?
Any major decisions as a kid that you remember making?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 20 --

Did you have a specific way of relating to the world that was
particular to you like being a observer, a partyer, a adventurer, a
connector, a book worm, a philosopher, being scientific,
planning everything strategically, a creative or a romantic?
What was the transition to college or work like? Did you leave
home then?
Were there any major relationships or themes in your twenties?
Did you have any strong childhood patterns or expectations that
changed and shifted?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 20 --

Relationship Questions
Tell me about any important boyfriends, girlfriends, or
when you met your spouse.
How would you describe falling in love with them?
Tell me about the early years of the relationship.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 20 --

Forward Transitions In Life Story
Let’s skip ahead now.
I have a great idea of who you were then. Now let’s fast
forward to age 30.
Give me the 1 minute description of your college years.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 20 --

Questions for the middle years
What are your favorite things to do
on the weekends during this phase
of life?
What are you like at work?
How would your family describe
you?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 20 --

Life Story Scramble
Tell me your life story in 1 minute. Use your hands and
your body. Stand up while you do it. So if your talking
about being a baby, cradle a baby. If you’re describing a
house, use your hands to shape a house. This kind of
thing.
Now tell it to me again but balance on one foot or while I
play this music.
Now lets move into fast speed. Tell me the same story in
30 seconds.
Now lets do the story in 15 seconds.
Now lets do the story in reverse.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com
